planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for striped white blooms.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil in full sun. The white to pale lilac flowers are feathered purple-brown on the outer petals with a yellow throat. This variable species is early flowering and suited to rock gardens and warm banks.

planting.note: Plant in fall for late, feathered lilac blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun. The lilac flowers are conspicuously feathered purple and buff on the outside with a yellow throat. Native to Corsica, it flowers a little later than most spring species and enjoys a warm, dry rest in summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for tiny late-spring flowers.

directions: Set the small corms about two inches deep in very sharp drainage in full sun. This dwarf species from Corsica and Sardinia has lilac flowers striped rich purple on the reverse. Best grown in a trough or raised bed where its tiny scale can be appreciated.

planting.note: Plant in fall for white blooms with purple veining.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. The white flowers are finely pencilled with deep purple veins on the outside, giving a delicate netted effect. It flowers in early to mid spring and thrives in warm, dry rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for large blue autumn blooms.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil as soon as they are available in late summer. The large violet-blue flowers are boldly veined and appear before the leaves in autumn. It naturalizes freely in grass and light woodland and is one of the easiest fall crocus to grow.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for deep sky-blue autumn flowers.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. This selection carries large, clear sky-blue flowers that are among the best blues of any autumn crocus. Leafless flowers rise straight from the ground in autumn and multiply over time.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for white autumn blooms.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This white form of the showy autumn crocus has a soft golden throat and a delicate look. The leafless flowers appear in autumn and combine beautifully with the blue-flowered species.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for pale lilac autumn flowers.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. The pale rosy-lilac flowers each have a pair of orange-yellow spots at the base of the petals. It flowers early in autumn and naturalizes well in sunny, well-drained banks.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for veined lilac-blue autumn blooms.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil in late summer. The soft lilac-blue flowers are veined with darker blue and carry a yellow throat and orange anthers. It flowers in autumn, tolerates light shade, and naturalizes gently over the years.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for iris-like autumn blooms.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in humus-rich soil that does not dry out badly in summer, in sun or light shade. The distinctive lilac flowers have three large outer petals and three small inner ones, giving an iris-like look. Unlike most crocus it enjoys a slightly cooler, moister site and flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for long-tubed lilac autumn blooms.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in a warm, sharply drained sunny spot in late summer. The rounded soft lilac flowers sit on long slender tubes and have a gentle fragrance. Native to southern Greece, it needs a hot, dry summer rest and does well in raised beds.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for deep purple autumn blooms.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in moist but well-drained soil in sun or light shade. The rich deep purple flowers rise leafless on slender tubes in autumn. It spreads by underground stolons and naturalizes well in grass that is not too dry.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for the wild parent of saffron.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in a hot, sharply drained sunny position in late summer. This wild Greek species is the ancestor of cultivated saffron and bears lilac flowers with long red stigmas. It flowers in autumn and needs a dry, warm summer dormancy to thrive.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for fragrant lilac autumn blooms.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in warm, sharply drained soil in full sun in late summer. The fragrant lilac flowers have a yellow throat and feathered exteriors and open late in autumn. Native to southern Italy and Sicily, it appreciates a hot, dry summer rest.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for fragrant late fall to winter blooms.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in a warm, very well-drained sunny spot in late summer. The fragrant white to lilac flowers are feathered purple on the outside and can bloom from late autumn into winter in mild areas. This Greek species is excellent for a bulb frame or sheltered raised bed.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn several weeks before the ground freezes so roots establish before winter.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep and 4 to 6 inches apart, pointed end up, in well-drained soil enriched with compost. Water once at planting and again as growth resumes in late winter, then let the soil dry between rains. Dense, intensely fragrant flower spikes open in mid-spring; snip spent stalks but leave the foliage until it yellows.

planting.note: Plant in fall while soil is still workable and cooling.

directions: Bury each bulb roughly 5 inches deep in loose, fast-draining soil, spacing them a hand's width apart. Keep the bed evenly moist during active spring growth and avoid summer irrigation once leaves fade. Soft porcelain-blue florets open in mid-spring with a strong sweet scent; remove faded spikes but ripen the leaves fully.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in autumn before hard frost.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot and firm the soil around them. Water in at planting, then rely on natural moisture until spikes push up in spring. Deep navy-blue florets with a thin paler stripe open densely in mid-spring; cut the flower stem after bloom and let foliage die back naturally.

planting.note: Fall-plant while the ground can still be dug.

directions: Position bulbs 5 inches deep and about 5 inches apart in rich, well-drained ground. Provide steady moisture as leaves emerge and taper off watering after flowering. Crisp pure-white spikes open in mid-spring and are excellent for cutting; deadhead spent stalks but keep the green leaves until they wither.

planting.note: Plant in autumn to allow a cold rooting period.

directions: Set bulbs roughly 5 inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil and water once to settle them. Keep the bed lightly moist through spring growth, then dry over summer dormancy. Soft primrose-yellow florets, a rare color for hyacinths, open fragrant in mid-spring; trim spent flowers and let foliage yellow before removal.

planting.note: Fall-plant several weeks ahead of freezing weather.

directions: Plant each bulb about 5 inches deep with the tip up, spacing a hand's width apart. Water at planting and during spring growth, then withhold moisture as the leaves die down. Bright rose-pink florets edged in a paler tone open densely in mid-spring; cut the stalk after flowering and allow the foliage to mature.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in fall before the soil freezes solid.

directions: Bury bulbs about 5 inches deep in sun and sharp drainage, firming soil around each. Keep moisture even while shoots and buds develop, then let the ground dry after bloom. Rich beetroot-purple florets form a plump fragrant spike in mid-spring; remove spent flowers but keep leaves growing until they naturally brown.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs 5 inches deep and about 5 inches apart in loose, well-drained soil. Water in at planting and again through spring emergence, then reduce watering as leaves fade. Vivid carmine-red florets open in mid-spring on a compact, strongly scented spike; deadhead after bloom and ripen the foliage fully.

planting.note: Fall-plant while soil remains workable.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 5 inches deep in fertile ground with good drainage. Give even moisture during active growth and let the bed dry through summer dormancy. Warm salmon-apricot florets open fragrant in mid-spring; snip the finished stalk and leave the leaves to feed the bulb.

planting.note: Plant in autumn before hard freezes arrive.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep and a hand's width apart in sun and good drainage. Water at planting and through spring growth, then keep dry after the leaves brown. Soft light-pink florets open early to mid-spring with a sweet fragrance; remove spent spikes and let the foliage ripen.

planting.note: Autumn planting gives the needed cold period.

directions: Plant bulbs 5 inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and settle with water. Maintain even moisture as growth begins in late winter, then dry down over summer. This heirloom bears pure ivory-white florets in mid-spring; deadhead the stalk and keep the leaves until they yellow.

planting.note: Fall-plant ahead of ground freeze.

directions: Bury bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sunny bed with sharp drainage. Water once at planting and during spring shoot growth, then leave the soil dry through dormancy. Deep indigo-blue florets pack a heavy scented spike in mid-spring; cut back finished flowers and let the foliage mature.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for a spring show.

directions: Set bulbs 5 inches deep and 5 inches apart in loose, fertile soil. Keep evenly moist during active growth, then withhold water as leaves fade. Clear soft-pink florets open in mid-spring on a neat fragrant spike; deadhead after bloom and allow the leaves to ripen fully.

planting.note: Fall-plant while the soil is still open.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 5 inches deep in sun with well-drained soil, firming them in place. Water at planting and through spring, then keep dry over summer rest. Large candy-pink florets form a full, fragrant spike in mid-spring; remove spent stalks but leave foliage to feed next year's bloom.

planting.note: Plant in fall before the ground freezes.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep and space them a hand's width apart in draining soil. Provide even moisture as leaves and buds develop, then dry down after flowering. Clean white florets open in mid-spring on a dense, sweetly scented spike; cut spent flowers and ripen the foliage before tidying.

planting.note: Autumn-plant for spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs 5 inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground and water to settle. Keep the bed moist through spring growth, then let it dry as leaves brown. Deep violet-purple florets open fragrant in mid-spring; deadhead the finished spike and leave the leaves until they yellow.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn several weeks before frost sets in.

directions: Bury bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed. Water at planting and through active spring growth, then leave dry over dormancy. Very dark blue-purple florets form a heavy fragrant spike in mid-spring; cut the stem after bloom and keep foliage growing until it fades.

planting.note: Fall-plant while the soil can still be worked.

directions: Set bulbs roughly 5 inches deep and 5 inches apart in loose fertile soil. Water in and keep evenly moist during spring, then dry down after flowering. Soft lilac-blue florets open in mid-spring on a full, scented spike; remove spent flowers and let leaves ripen naturally.

planting.note: Plant in fall for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in sun with free-draining soil. Water at planting and during spring growth, then hold off as leaves fade. Clear mid-blue florets form a dense fragrant spike in mid-spring; deadhead spent stalks and allow the foliage to mature before removal.

planting.note: Autumn-plant to allow winter rooting.

directions: Set bulbs 5 inches deep and a hand's width apart in fertile, well-drained soil. Keep evenly moist as growth resumes, then dry down for summer dormancy. Warm buttery-yellow florets open fragrant in mid-spring; snip spent spikes and leave the leaves to feed the bulb.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; this looser type naturalizes better than florist hyacinths.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 4 inches deep in well-drained soil, spacing them a few inches apart. Water to establish and keep lightly moist in spring, then allow summer dryness. Each bulb sends up several slender, loosely flowered stems of fragrant blooms in early to mid-spring; leave the clumps undisturbed to spread over the years.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in a warm, sharply drained rock-garden spot.

directions: Set the bulbs about 4 inches deep in gritty, very well-drained soil that bakes dry in summer. Water sparingly during spring growth and avoid wet conditions during dormancy. This wild species opens loose spikes of pale blue, spidery-petalled florets in early spring; leave it undisturbed to form a slow clump.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a lean, free-draining site.

directions: Plant bulbs around 4 inches deep in gritty soil with excellent drainage and full sun. Keep barely moist in spring and dry through the summer rest. This dwarf wild species carries short spikes of nodding, waxy blue bells in early spring; it suits troughs and raised alpine beds where it stays dry.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn; they root quickly and naturalize freely.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in groups for the best effect. Grassy leaves often appear in fall, which is normal, followed by dense cobalt-blue flower spikes in early to mid-spring. Leave clumps to multiply and self-sow; lift and divide only when crowding reduces bloom.

planting.note: Fall-plant in drifts for a soft haze of color.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in well-drained soil. Water to establish in fall and enjoy the icy pale-blue spikes in mid-spring. This selection has an unusually muted, powder-blue tone; leave clumps to spread and divide only when they crowd.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for the fullest double spikes.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 3 inches deep in fertile, draining soil, grouping them closely. Keep lightly moist during spring growth, then allow summer dryness. This double form bears fat, cone-shaped spikes packed with many small sky-blue florets in mid-spring, lasting well as it is largely sterile; leave clumps to bulk up.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in drifts or borders.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in well-drained ground. Water to establish and let the dense spikes emerge in mid-spring. Deep navy-blue florets are each rimmed with a crisp white mouth, giving a strong two-tone look; leave the plants to naturalize.

planting.note: Plant in fall for spring color.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in draining soil. Keep lightly moist through spring growth, then allow the bed to dry in summer. Bright blue florets edged with a distinct white rim form tidy spikes in mid-spring; leave clumps undisturbed to increase.

planting.note: Fall-plant in a well-drained border.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and space closely in groups. Water to establish and enjoy the unusually long-lasting spikes in mid to late spring. This double selection opens blue and ages toward green-tinted tones, giving a shifting two-color effect; leave clumps to bulk up over time.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in draining soil. Keep lightly moist during spring, then dry down in summer. Soft blue-and-white florets give a cool minty look on neat spikes in mid-spring; leave clumps to naturalize.

planting.note: Fall-plant; this early type often blooms ahead of others.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in well-drained soil. Water to establish in fall and watch for early spikes of rich violet-blue florets, among the first grape hyacinths to open. Leave clumps to multiply and naturalize.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; this old-garden species naturalizes readily.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in ordinary well-drained soil. Water to settle and let the short, rounded spikes of tight blue bells appear in mid-spring. This hardy heirloom self-sows and spreads easily; leave undisturbed to form colonies.

planting.note: Fall-plant this white form among blue types for contrast.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and space closely in groups. Water to establish and enjoy short spikes of tightly packed pure-white bells in mid-spring. Slower to increase than the blue kinds but long-lived; leave clumps undisturbed and divide only when crowded.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; each bulb makes a single wide leaf.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in well-drained soil. Each bulb produces one broad, tulip-like leaf and a distinctive two-tone spike in mid-spring, with pale sky-blue sterile flowers crowning dark blackish-blue fertile ones. Leave clumps to establish and increase slowly.

planting.note: Fall-plant; this species is neat and non-invasive.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in draining soil. Water to establish and enjoy compact spikes in mid-spring. Bright blue florets tipped with a small white mouth top short, tidy stems; this species stays put rather than seeding around, so it suits refined plantings.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in well-drained soil. Water to settle in fall and let the spikes rise in mid-spring. Each spike shades from pale, almost white florets at the top to deeper sky-blue below, giving a soft gradient; leave clumps to increase gently.

planting.note: Fall-plant this white selection for cool contrast.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in draining soil. Water to establish and enjoy short, neat spikes in mid-spring. Florets open pale blue-tinged and mature to clean white, crowning tidy stems; this well-behaved species pairs nicely with blue forms.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; this species flowers early and low.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 inches deep and a couple of inches apart in gritty, well-drained soil. Water to establish and look for dense, dumpy spikes of clear sky-blue bells in early spring. Its open, upward-facing florets are a paler true blue than most; leave to naturalize in rock gardens and edges.

planting.note: Plant in autumn, but site with care as it can spread vigorously.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep in ordinary, well-drained soil. Water to settle and enjoy very dark blue-black florets rimmed in white on short spikes in mid-spring. This vigorous species self-sows and offsets freely, so place it where a spreading colony is welcome or deadhead to limit seeding.

planting.note: Fall-plant; this tassel type blooms later than other Muscari.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 to 4 inches deep and a few inches apart in well-drained soil. Water to establish and enjoy the unusual bloom in late spring, when each stem carries a fluffy plume of sterile, thread-like violet filaments instead of normal bells. It is sterile and spreads only by offsets; leave clumps to bulk up.

planting.note: Fall-plant in a warm, very well-drained, sunny spot.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 inches deep in sharply drained, gritty soil that stays dry in summer. Water lightly during spring growth only. Purple buds open to fragrant yellow florets banded with brown in mid to late spring, releasing a sweet scent; give it the warmest, driest spot you have and protect from winter wet.

planting.note: Fall-plant this rare pink form in a bright, draining spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in well-drained soil. Water to establish and enjoy short spikes in mid-spring. This unusual selection opens soft pink florets rather than the familiar blue, giving a gentle pastel effect; leave clumps undisturbed to slowly increase.

planting.note: Plant in fall for tall early-summer flowers.

directions: Set bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep in a sunny bed with sharp drainage. The strong stems carry 5- to 6-inch lilac-purple spheres that rise well above surrounding perennials. Interplant with lower foliage plants to hide the leaves, which yellow as the flowers open.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn while the soil is still workable.

directions: Plant each bulb 6 to 8 inches deep in free-draining ground. This long-lived hybrid holds dense purple globes on sturdy stems that resist flopping. Flowers open in early summer and keep their form as they age to a papery tan.

planting.note: Autumn planting for tall white summer globes.

directions: Bury bulbs about 7 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained site. Large ivory-white spheres glow against darker plantings in early summer. Combine with purple alliums for contrast and leave the heads to dry in place.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 to 6 inches deep in gritty, sharply drained soil. Enormous open heads of metallic star-shaped florets can reach 8 to 10 inches across on short stems. The airy seed heads dry beautifully and last for months in the garden or a vase.

planting.note: Plant in fall; mulch in colder zones.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 to 6 inches deep in warm, very well-drained soil. The flower head explodes into a firework of florets on stems of uneven length, spanning up to a foot across. Dried heads break free like tumbleweeds and are prized for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; excellent for the front of a border.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in very well-drained soil. Broad blue-green leaves fan out below globes of soft pink-silver florets in late spring. Its low, bold foliage makes it a standout at a border's edge.

planting.note: Autumn planting for low white spring flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 inches deep in sharply drained soil at the front of a bed. Wide ribbed leaves frame dense heads of creamy-white star florets in late spring. Keep the crown dry through summer dormancy for best rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild-winter areas.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in sunny, well-drained soil. Loose domes of soft pink flowers open in late spring above narrow leaves. It spreads freely in warm climates, so give it room where naturalizing is welcome.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; prefers milder winters.

directions: Set bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Airy clusters of clear rose-pink flowers appear in late spring on wiry stems. A West Coast native, it appreciates a drier rest once the foliage fades.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in well-drained soil. Broad flat-topped heads of white flowers, each with a dark green center eye, open in late spring. Despite the name the blooms are white, and they hold well as cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; ideal for rock gardens.

directions: Plant these tiny bulbs about 2 to 3 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Loose umbels of bright carmine-pink flowers hug the ground in late spring. It is perfect for rock gardens, troughs, and the front of a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild regions for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in a sunny spot. Fragrant loose clusters of pure-white star flowers appear in spring. It makes an excellent scented cut flower and naturalizes in mild-winter gardens.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for mid-summer flowers.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in lean, sharply drained soil. Dangling clusters of soft-yellow bells open in mid to late summer above blue-gray foliage. It thrives in hot, dry sites where many bulbs would fail.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew, in well-drained soil, spacing them to form tidy clumps. Rounded lavender-pink flower heads rise above blue-green foliage in mid to late summer. This clump-forming species is drought-tolerant and beloved by bees.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed and water until established. This tidy clumping hybrid sends up masses of rounded rose-purple flowers in mid to late summer. Named a Perennial Plant of the Year, it draws bees and butterflies and needs little care.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown just at the surface in average, well-drained soil. Neat mounds of glossy strap leaves stay attractive all season and hold soft lavender-pink globes in midsummer. It is sterile, non-weedy, and a reliable pollinator plant.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Spidery star-shaped florets form loose violet heads with a shimmering, streaked look. Blooming in late spring, it pairs well with rounder allium types for textural contrast.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Deep maroon-red drumstick heads are topped with a tuft of pale green florets like a mohawk. This quirky bloom appears in early summer and is a favorite for cutting.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot. Cone-shaped burgundy heads are crowned by a spray of tiny green-tipped florets. The unusual tufted flowers open in early summer and add height and whimsy to borders.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring globes.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 7 inches deep in well-drained soil. Dense violet-purple globes up to 7 inches across sit on relatively short, sturdy stems. The large heads open in late spring and dry into lasting ornaments.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 7 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Large rounded purple heads rise on tall stems in early summer. Interplant with leafy perennials to disguise the fading basal foliage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil. Big rosy-purple globes bloom in early summer on strong stems that stand up to wind. Leave the spent heads for architectural interest through the season.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot. Deep burgundy-purple drumstick heads bloom from early to midsummer, later than most tall alliums. The rich color weaves beautifully through ornamental grasses.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 7 inches deep in well-drained soil. Rounded white heads are accented by a deep maroon-red eye at the center of each floret. The two-tone globes open in late spring and make striking cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 to 4 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed. Open heads of white star flowers, each with a dark central stripe, appear in late spring. This delicate, long-blooming allium is lovely at the front of a border.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed. Blue-tinged foliage forms a tidy mound topped with rosy-purple flower globes in mid to late summer. This clumping hybrid resists deer and rabbits and feeds late-season pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level in well-drained ground. Twisting, curly blue-green leaves form a swirling mound below rosy-pink summer flowers. Its drought tolerance and unusual foliage make it a distinctive edging plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for late-summer bloom.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface in a sunny, well-drained spot. Deep reddish-purple flower heads rise above glossy green clumping foliage in late summer. It blooms later than many alliums, extending the pollinator season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Set divisions at soil level in gritty, well-drained ground. Bicolor flowers open pale pink with a rosy stripe and shift shades as they age through summer. This compact clumping allium is well suited to the front of a border or a container.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in sharply drained soil. Flattened domes of very dark maroon-purple florets bloom in early summer on tall stems. The deep, almost black color adds moody contrast among brighter alliums.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in groups in well-drained soil. Rounded lilac-purple heads about 3 to 4 inches across open in late spring. A dependable naturalizer, it self-sows gently and returns for many years.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 7 inches deep in a sunny site with excellent drainage. Large rounded lilac-purple globes rise on tall stems in early summer. Keep the bulbs dry during their summer dormancy to encourage strong reflowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level in well-drained ground. Compact mounds of grassy foliage carry soft pink pompom flowers in summer. Its small size suits containers, edging, and rock gardens, and it draws many bees.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Set divisions at soil level in gritty, well-drained soil. Swirled powder-blue foliage forms a compact mound below rosy-pink flower heads in late summer. Drought-tolerant and neat, it is excellent for edging and troughs.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer flowers.

directions: Set bulbs 7 to 8 inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil. Very large rose-purple globes bloom for an unusually long stretch in early summer. Interplant with perennials to conceal the leaves as they fade.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in sunny, sharply drained soil. Full violet-purple globes open in late spring, among the earlier tall alliums to bloom. Give the bulbs a dry summer rest for the best return the following year.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer to early fall so a basal leaf rosette forms before winter.

directions: Set bulbs shallowly with only about an inch of soil over the nose, unlike most lilies which are planted deep. Give this one alkaline, well-drained soil and leave it undisturbed once established. Fragrant pure-white trumpets open in early summer above the previous autumn's overwintered foliage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn or early spring about three times their height deep.

directions: Bury bulbs six to eight inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and stake tall stems in exposed sites. This trumpet species is one of the easiest lilies to grow and even flowers reliably from seed within a few years. Large white trumpets flushed wine-purple outside open in midsummer with a heady evening perfume.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in acid, humus-rich soil; one of the latest lilies to bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in moist, acidic, well-drained soil with a little afternoon shade in hot regions. Blooming very late in the season, it extends the lily display into early autumn. Fragrant, strongly recurved white flowers heavily brushed with crimson-pink nod on wiry stems.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in acid soil with excellent drainage; can be short-lived in gardens.

directions: Set bulbs deeply in sharply drained, lime-free soil rich in leaf mold, as they rot easily in wet ground. This spectacular Japanese species can produce enormous bowl-shaped blooms on well-grown stems. Huge fragrant white flowers with a gold ray down each petal and crimson speckles open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in consistently moist, humus-rich soil at a woodland edge.

directions: Plant bulbs four to six inches deep in rich, moist soil that never fully dries out, mimicking its native wet meadows. This is our tallest native lily and can carry dozens of flowers on an established stem. Nodding orange turk's-cap blooms with a green star in the throat appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant garden bulbs in fall, or set out a forced pot plant after bloom to naturalize.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in well-drained soil, and gift-plant bulbs can be moved to the garden once the flowers fade. In the garden it blooms in summer rather than at Easter, since holiday plants are forced under glass. Fragrant, pure-white outward-facing trumpets open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in gritty, well-drained soil; short-lived but easy from seed.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three inches deep in sharply drained soil; this dwarf species is quick and easy to raise from seed. Its compact size suits the front of a border or a rock garden. Glossy, small, recurved scarlet turk's-cap flowers dangle on slender stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained soil; tolerates summer dryness once established.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in well-drained woodland or meadow soil in dappled sun. Native to the Pacific Northwest, it copes with drier summers better than the eastern wetland lilies. Small nodding orange turk's-cap flowers with dark spots hang from open stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; some forms produce stem bulbils for easy propagation.

directions: Set bulbs five to six inches deep in ordinary well-drained garden soil in a sunny spot. This hardy European species is undemanding and stands upright without much staking. Erect, cup-shaped orange flowers flecked with dark spots face the sky in early summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained soil; an important parent of Asiatic hybrids.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This tough Chinese species contributed its vigor and spotting to countless modern Asiatic hybrids. Numerous small, recurved cinnabar-orange turk's-cap flowers with black spots appear in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in gritty, well-drained soil; easy from seed but often short-lived.

directions: Set the small bulbs three inches deep in sharply drained soil in a sunny rock garden or border front. Compact and refined, this species is prized for its unusual color among lilies. Small, fragrant, recurved lilac-pink flowers with fine dark dots nod on wiry stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in humus-rich soil with light shade; tolerates part shade well.

directions: Plant bulbs four to six inches deep in rich, well-drained woodland soil in dappled light. Sturdy and early-flowering, it is a key parent of the shade-tolerant Martagon hybrids. Thick-petaled, nodding orange-yellow turk's-cap flowers with brown spots open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a sheltered spot; the stem wanders underground before emerging.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in moisture-retentive but sharply drained soil in a warm, sheltered position. The stem often travels sideways underground, so allow it room to surface a foot or more from the bulb. Striking pendent flowers of pale green-yellow with a deep maroon throat appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in acid, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in rich, well-drained, slightly acid soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This famous Oriental hybrid is prized as a cut flower and faces its blooms upward rather than nodding. Intensely fragrant crimson-pink flowers edged white and dotted with darker spots open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in acid, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in fertile, well-drained acid soil and provide support in windy sites. A classic pure-white Oriental beloved for weddings and cutting. Very large, powerfully fragrant, pure-white outward-facing flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in acid, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in rich, slightly acid soil that drains freely. This reliable Oriental hybrid combines strong fragrance with a soft, ruffled look. Broad white flowers flecked pink and speckled with red spots open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in acid, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in humus-rich, freely draining acid soil and mulch the root zone. This popular Oriental is admired for its crisp bicolor markings and strong scent. White petals with a broad raspberry-red central band and scattered spots open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring; a dwarf type well suited to pots.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in well-drained acid soil or a container of gritty mix. Its short stature makes this fragrant Oriental ideal for patio pots and border fronts. Large pink flowers edged white with darker spots open on compact stems in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in well-drained soil; blooms early in the lily season.

directions: Set bulbs five to six inches deep in ordinary well-drained soil in full sun. This classic old Asiatic hybrid is exceptionally easy, multiplies readily, and needs no staking. Upward-facing, unscented nasturtium-orange flowers with fine dark spots open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in full sun and good drainage.

directions: Plant bulbs five to six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in a sunny bed. A dependable and vigorous Asiatic that stands well without support and makes a long-lasting cut flower. Upward-facing, clear golden-yellow flowers open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun for the deepest color. Famed as one of the darkest lilies available, it makes a dramatic contrast against paler flowers. Upward-facing flowers of deep blackish-maroon-red open on tall stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in well-drained garden soil in full sun. This vigorous Asiatic carries many small pendent flowers and multiplies quickly into generous clumps. Numerous nodding, recurved lemon-yellow flowers with black spots open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs five to six inches deep in well-drained soil in a sunny spot. This double-flowered Asiatic has no pollen, so it will not stain clothing or drop messy anthers. Fully double, upward-facing golden-yellow flowers with light spotting open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil and stake the tall stems. This trumpet hybrid is powerfully fragrant and best sited where its evening scent can be enjoyed. Large, outward-facing apricot-orange trumpets flushed bronze outside open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs eight inches deep in deep, humus-rich soil and support the towering stems. This trumpet strain is one of the tallest and most fragrant lilies for the summer border. Large, richly scented deep pink-purple trumpets open on stately stems in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and stake against wind. This trumpet strain is vigorous and intensely fragrant, glowing in the evening garden. Large golden-yellow trumpets stained maroon on the reverse open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring three times their height deep.

directions: Bury bulbs six to eight inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and stake tall stems if exposed. This pure white selection of the regal lily lacks the purple exterior of the species. Fragrant, glistening white trumpets with a yellow throat open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs eight inches deep in deep, fertile soil that drains well and leave undisturbed to build size. This robust, long-lived hybrid can carry dozens of flowers on a mature stem and rarely needs replacing. Recurved deep crimson-red flowers edged white with a green center open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs eight inches deep in deep, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun. This vigorous Orienpet grows very tall and holds its many blooms for a long season with only light fragrance. Large outward-facing flowers of deep red edged and tipped in gold open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs eight inches deep in deep, fertile soil with good drainage and full sun. This sturdy Orienpet produces enormous, lightly scented blooms and thick stems that seldom need staking. Very large, bowl-shaped pale yellow flowers with a soft gold center open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs eight inches deep in deep, humus-rich soil and give this tall grower room to reach full height. A striking Orienpet, it can tower well above eye level on an established clump. Large, gently recurved raspberry-pink flowers edged white with darker spots open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs eight inches deep in deep, well-drained soil enriched with compost in full sun. This dependable Orienpet grows tall and fragrant, forming a bold late-summer focal point. Large outward-facing white flowers with a deep raspberry-red star in the throat open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. This graceful aurelian hybrid descends from Lilium henryi and carries airy sprays of many blooms. Nodding, strongly recurved white flowers with an apricot-orange center open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply; stem bulbils aid propagation.

directions: Set bulbs six to eight inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered spot. Closely related to the regal lily, it forms bulbils in the leaf axils that can be grown on. Fragrant white trumpets flushed purple-brown outside with a yellow throat open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil and stake the tall stems. This vigorous Chinese species is another parent behind many garden trumpet hybrids. Large fragrant creamy-white trumpets flushed greenish-yellow in the throat open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and stake tall stems in open sites. This trumpet strain is prized for its warm color and powerful evening fragrance. Large deep golden-apricot trumpets stained brownish outside open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs eight inches deep in deep, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun. This towering Orienpet produces very large blooms on thick, self-supporting stems. Huge outward-facing deep raspberry-red flowers with a paler edge open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring deeply in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This large Orienpet grows tall with sturdy stems and huge flowers that make a bold statement. Very large, flat-faced deep purple-red flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant corms after all danger of frost has passed, staggering plantings for a longer bloom season.

directions: Set corms about four inches deep with the pointed end up in fertile, well-drained soil. Plant new batches every two weeks through late spring to spread out the flower spikes, and stake tall varieties before they lean. Cut spikes for the vase when the lowest one or two florets show color, and lift corms after the first fall frost in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant corms four to five inches deep in a warm, sunny bed with sharp drainage. This vigorous African species naturalizes in mild climates and carries hooded red-and-yellow flowers on arching stems. Expect bloom in mid to late summer, and leave clumps undisturbed where winters stay mild.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost once soil is warm; often started indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot after the soil warms. This fragrant species, once sold as Acidanthera, bears nodding white flowers with a deep maroon central blotch on wiry stems. Blooms arrive in late summer, and corms should be lifted and stored dry over winter outside mild zones.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn where winters are mild for spring flowering.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in fall in a well-drained bed; this winter-growing South African species prefers moisture in the cool season and dry summers. Slender stems carry creamy-yellow flowers that release a sweet scent in the evening. Flowering comes in spring, after which the plant goes dormant for the summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates for late spring bloom.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in fall. This South African species bears brilliant scarlet flowers each marked with a white spear on the lower petals. It flowers in late spring and should be kept on the dry side once foliage fades for its summer rest.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in frost-free or nearly frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in fall in sandy, sharply drained soil. Soft pink to white flowers, often blotched with red on the lower petals, open on slender spikes in spring. Allow a dry summer dormancy, watering again only when growth resumes in autumn.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost, in succession for continued bloom.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil once frost has passed. This classic cultivar carries large ruffled apricot-salmon florets with a small red throat on tall spikes. Cut spikes for arrangements as the first florets color up, and lift corms for winter storage in cold zones.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost date.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun after frost. 'Atom' is prized for its bright scarlet florets neatly outlined with a thin silvery-white picotee edge. It blooms in mid to late summer; stake if needed and cut for the vase as the lower buds open.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost for late-summer spikes.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in spring. This cultivar bears tall spikes of clear chartreuse-green florets that are popular with florists. Provide steady moisture during growth and lift the corms after the foliage is frosted in colder climates.

planting.note: Plant corms in warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in a sunny bed after the last frost. 'Nova Lux' produces large, pure golden-yellow florets stacked on strong upright stems. It flowers in summer and makes an excellent cut flower; harvest spikes when the lowest floret begins to open.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost, staggering for longer bloom.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in fertile soil in full sun. This popular cultivar carries big ruffled florets of creamy white edged in soft pink with a yellow throat. Blooms open in mid to late summer; cut early in the morning as the lowest buds show color for long vase life.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost in a sunny position.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in well-drained soil once frost has passed. 'Espresso' is grown for its deep, velvety dark-red florets that read almost black in the border. Stake tall spikes and cut for arrangements as the buds begin to open in summer.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last spring frost.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in a warm, sunny bed with good drainage. This cultivar bears large, ruffled florets in a rich purplish-maroon so dark it appears nearly black. It flowers in summer and pairs well with lighter shades; harvest spikes as the lower florets open.

planting.note: Plant corms in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. 'Plum Tart' offers deep wine-purple florets with a slight sheen on sturdy spikes. Blooms come in mid to late summer; stake if the site is windy and cut for the vase as color shows.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost for summer flowers.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed once the soil warms. 'Fidelio' produces ruffled florets in a rich violet-purple with paler markings toward the center. It flowers in summer; provide even moisture and lift the corms before hard frost in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile soil in full sun. 'Traderhorn' bears vivid fiery-red florets marked with a bold white flash on the lower petals. It is a strong, showy grower that flowers in summer and makes a striking cut flower.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost date.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in well-drained soil in a sunny spot. 'Video' produces soft smoky-mauve to dusky-rose florets with a subtle color wash that appeals to arrangers. It flowers in summer; harvest spikes as the lowest florets begin to open for the longest display.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost in warm soil.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile, sunny ground once frost has passed. 'Purple Flora' bears deep saturated purple florets on tall, well-filled spikes. It flowers in summer and lift corms for dry winter storage where the ground freezes.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last spring frost.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun. 'Vista' carries blended pink-and-cream florets with a soft, luminous look on strong stems. It blooms in summer; stagger plantings and cut spikes as the buds color for a succession of arrangements.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost for a summer show.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile soil in full sun. 'Jester' bears bright yellow florets accented by a red blotch in the throat for a cheerful contrast. It flowers in summer and makes a long-lasting cut flower harvested as the lower buds open.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed after frost. 'Wine and Roses' displays rosy-pink florets deepening to a wine-red throat for a two-tone effect. It blooms in summer; provide steady water during growth and cut spikes for the vase as color shows.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile soil in full sun. 'Far West' produces soft salmon-pink florets with a lightly ruffled edge on tall spikes. It flowers in summer; stake in exposed sites and lift corms before hard frost in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost for summer bloom.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun. 'Amsterdam' bears clean white florets on tall, elegant spikes prized by florists. It flowers in summer and makes a superb cut flower; harvest early as the lowest one or two florets begin to open.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost date.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed after frost. 'Zizanie' is distinctive for its red florets flecked and streaked with white in an irregular speckled pattern. It flowers in summer and adds a playful note to mixed bouquets.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost in warm soil.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. 'Cream Perfection' offers soft creamy-yellow florets that fade to near white for a gentle, subtle look. It flowers in summer and is an easy, reliable cut flower for the border.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild zones, or spring in cooler climates.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in a sheltered sunny spot. This dainty early-flowering hybrid bears pure white flowers on slender stems well before the large summer gladiolus. In mild regions plant in fall for late spring bloom, or start under glass in colder areas.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild zones for early-summer bloom.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in a warm, sunny position. 'Nymph' is a compact dwarf gladiolus bearing white flowers marked with a bright pink or red flash on the lower petals. It flowers earlier than large hybrids and is hardy enough to overwinter in the ground in milder gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates or spring elsewhere.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun. This dwarf 'Charm' bears rosy-pink flowers with contrasting markings on graceful, slender spikes. It blooms in early summer and naturalizes where winters are mild.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild zones for early bloom.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in a sunny, sheltered spot. 'Robinetta' is a compact species-type gladiolus with deep cerise-red flowers accented by a small cream mark. It flowers in early summer and is among the hardier gladiolus for perennializing in mild gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates or spring in cooler areas.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun. 'Prins Claus' bears elegant white flowers each brushed with a rose-pink teardrop on the lower petals. It is a graceful, early-flowering dwarf that suits the front of the border and cutting garden alike.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild zones for early-summer flowers.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun. 'Impressive' produces soft pale-pink flowers with deeper rose-pink markings on the lower petals. This dwarf gladiolus flowers early and multiplies happily where winters are mild.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost date.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. 'Halloween' bears warm burnt-orange to bronze florets on tall spikes for a rich autumnal color. It flowers in summer; stake if needed and lift corms for storage before frost in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild zones or spring elsewhere.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in a warm, sunny spot. 'Nathalie' bears soft salmon-pink flowers on slender, wiry stems that sway gracefully. This dwarf gladiolus flowers early and is well suited to the cutting garden and border edge.

planting.note: Plant when nighttime temperatures stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Set tubers six inches deep and install a sturdy stake at planting so you avoid spearing the roots later. The bright yellow dinner-plate blooms can reach ten inches across and need support to stay upright. Disbud side shoots to channel energy into fewer, larger flowers that appear from late summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant outdoors once the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Lay the tuber on its side about six inches deep and stake at planting time. The large peachy-pink and lavender blended blooms are a favorite of flower arrangers, so cut them fully open since they do not continue opening in the vase. Keep the plant well watered during hot spells for continuous flowering into fall.

planting.note: Plant tubers when the frost risk has fully passed.

directions: Plant six inches deep with a stout stake because this variety grows tall and carries enormous pink blooms sometimes over twelve inches across. Disbud aggressively to grow the record-size flowers the plant is known for. Cut stems early in the day and keep the plant fed and watered through the summer.

planting.note: Plant after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Set the tuber six inches deep in fertile soil and stake it to support the big blooms. This dinner-plate variety opens pure creamy-white flowers that glow in the evening garden and make dramatic cut flowers. Deadhead old blooms and lift the tubers before hard frost in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber horizontally about six inches deep and stake it right away. The soft pink dinner-plate blooms reach eight to ten inches across on vigorous plants that flower prolifically. Cut for arrangements when fully open and keep spent blooms removed to encourage more buds.

planting.note: Plant once soil temperatures rise in late spring.

directions: Bury the tuber six inches deep and provide strong support for the towering stems. The shaggy semi-cactus blooms shade from soft coral to creamy peach and can span nearly a foot. Disbud to grow the largest flowers and cut them just as they open for long-lasting displays.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil after frost.

directions: Set the tuber four to six inches deep with the eye upward. This orchid-type dahlia opens open, star-shaped single yellow blooms with narrow rolled petals that pollinators love. Deadhead often and cut stems for the vase as the flowers open through late summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Set the tuber six inches deep and stake at planting. This decorative dahlia blends coral, salmon, and bronze tones in blooms about six inches across on good cutting stems. Cut when fully open and remove faded flowers to keep buds coming through fall.

planting.note: Plant after frost into warmed soil.

directions: Plant the tuber four to six inches deep in full sun. This ball dahlia produces perfectly rounded blooms in warm salmon-orange that are excellent for cutting and long-lasting in arrangements. Keep the plant fed and watered, and pick flowers regularly to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Plant tubers when the soil has warmed after frost.

directions: Bury the tuber six inches deep and provide a strong stake. This classic dinner-plate variety opens huge pink blooms suffused with golden-yellow that can reach ten inches across. Disbud for maximum size and cut flowers as they fully open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost.

directions: Plant the tuber four to six inches deep in a sunny bed. This ball dahlia carries neat globe-shaped blooms in soft dusky pink prized by florists for weddings. Cut stems fully open in the morning and deadhead to keep the plant producing into autumn.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil once frost passes.

directions: Set the tuber six inches deep and stake at planting. The large dinner-plate blooms fade from soft lavender-pink to creamy white toward the center on tall, strong stems. Disbud for larger flowers and cut them just as they open for elegant arrangements.

planting.note: Plant after frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Plant the tuber six inches deep and support the tall stems with a stake. This semi-cactus dinner-plate dahlia shows rich purple petals brushed with white tips. Cut flowers as they open and keep the plant deadheaded through the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant four to six inches deep in a sunny position and stake for support. This decorative dahlia glows in warm orange tones that shift toward gold and works well both in the border and the vase. Deadhead spent blooms and cut fresh flowers regularly to extend the display.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn several weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up about six to eight inches deep in fall in well-drained soil. These large, sturdy tulips bloom in mid-spring on tall stems and return more reliably than most hybrids. Let the foliage yellow and die back naturally after flowering so the bulb can recharge for next year.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn before the soil freezes hard.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep and twice their own depth apart in fall. These classic golden trumpet daffodils naturalize well and are ignored by deer and rodents. Leave the leaves standing for at least six weeks after bloom so the bulbs build strength for the following spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn a month or two before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in fall in well-drained soil, wearing gloves since the bulbs can irritate skin. Dense spikes of intensely fragrant flowers appear in mid-spring in shades of blue, pink, white, and purple. The spikes shrink in later years, so many gardeners treat them as short-lived or replant fresh bulbs.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost and again every couple of weeks for continuous bloom.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in full sun and stake taller varieties to keep the flower spikes upright. Make successive plantings every two weeks into early summer for a long parade of blooms. Cut spikes when the lowest florets open, and in cold zones lift and dry the corms for winter storage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about three times their height deep in well-drained soil with the pointed end up. These early-summer lilies bloom in bright, unscented upward-facing flowers and multiply over the years. Leave the stems standing until they yellow in fall, then cut them back so the bulb can store energy.

planting.note: Plant soaked corms in early spring in cold zones or fall in mild ones.

directions: Soak the hard corms overnight, then plant them about two inches deep in cool, well-drained soil. The poppy-like single flowers open in vivid reds, blues, purples, and white with dark centers. Plant in fall for spring bloom in mild climates, and cut flowers freely as they open to encourage more.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring where winters are cold, or fall in frost-free areas.

directions: Plant corms pointed end up about two inches deep in well-drained soil and cool conditions. The arching stems carry intensely fragrant funnel-shaped flowers in a wide range of colors, superb for cutting. Provide support for the wiry stems, and lift the corms after the foliage dies back in areas with frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three to four inches deep in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot. These jewel-toned miniature iris bloom very early, often pushing up through the last snow in late winter. Plant them in rock gardens or near paths where their small size and fragrance can be appreciated up close.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in early autumn as they dislike drying out.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about three inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich soil, ideally while still fresh. Nodding white bell flowers with green markings appear in late winter, often the first blooms of the year. They naturalize best in light shade under deciduous trees and can be divided just after flowering while still in leaf.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after the worst frost has passed.

directions: Plant corms three to five inches deep in spring in full sun and well-drained soil. Arching sword-shaped leaves give way to fiery scarlet flowers on wiry stems in mid to late summer that hummingbirds love. Leave clumps to bulk up over several years and mulch in the coldest zones for winter protection.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant rhizomes about two to three inches deep with the growth buds facing up in rich, moist soil. The elegant funnel-shaped spathes come in white, yellow, pink, and near-black on clean upright stems ideal for cutting. Keep the soil consistently moist during growth, and lift and dry the rhizomes for storage where winters freeze.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in autumn before the soil freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in fall in well-drained soil, grouping them for impact. Upward-facing blue star flowers with white centers open in early spring as the snow retreats. Easy and cold-hardy, it naturalizes readily by self-seeding into cheerful early-season drifts.

planting.note: Set rhizomes out once soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant rhizomes about 4 inches deep with the eyes facing up in rich, moist soil. The hard round seeds need nicking and soaking before they will sprout. Expect slender red or orange blooms from midsummer until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Set out in boggy ground or shallow water once spring warms.

directions: This native wetland canna thrives in mud and shallow standing water. Plant rhizomes just below the surface and keep them saturated. Large, delicate yellow flowers open in early summer and are a favorite of pollinators.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in deep, rich soil with room to grow tall.

directions: Give this towering species deep, fertile soil and steady moisture to reach its full height. Nodding clusters of rosy-pink flowers dangle from arching stems in late summer. Stake in windy sites and mulch heavily where winters dip below its hardiness range.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost where the huge leaves have space and shelter.

directions: Grown mainly for its enormous banana-like leaves on red-tinged stems, this canna makes a bold tropical accent. Plant rhizomes 4 to 6 inches deep in rich soil and feed generously through summer. Small orange flowers may appear late in the season but the foliage is the real show.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: A longtime favorite with broad green leaves and large scarlet-red flowers. Set rhizomes 4 inches deep in fertile, moist soil and water freely in hot weather. Remove spent flower spikes to keep new blooms coming until frost.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in warm, moist ground.

directions: This novelty canna is prized for its unpredictable blooms that mix yellow, red-spotted, and solid red flowers on the same plant. Leaves may show green and bronze streaking to match. Plant rhizomes shallowly in rich soil and enjoy the surprise of each opening flower.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost for dark foliage all summer.

directions: Deep bronze-purple leaves make a striking backdrop for its ruffled tangerine-orange flowers. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in full sun to keep the foliage darkest. This tall, vigorous variety works well at the back of a border.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost where the striped leaves will catch the light.

directions: Also sold as Bengal Tiger, this canna has green leaves boldly striped in yellow with a fine red edge. Bright orange flowers rise above the dramatic foliage. Plant rhizomes in rich, moist soil and give plenty of sun for the best leaf color.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost for vivid multicolor foliage.

directions: Sold under the Tropicanna name, this variety shows leaves striped in burgundy, pink, gold, and green that glow when backlit. Fiery orange flowers top the stems in summer. Plant rhizomes shallowly and site in full sun to bring out every foliage color.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a sunny spot.

directions: Green leaves striped with bright gold pair with orange flowers edged in yellow. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in fertile soil and keep evenly moist. Deadhead finished spikes to encourage a long run of bloom.
